ops.title: Destructive daemon behaviors (eviction) must be opt-in consumer config; excavate fleet prior art before designing daemonkit behavior
ops.body:
While planning daemonkit's protocol-versioning removal (2026-07-19), I proposed making takeover auto-evict mismatched-protocol incumbents unconditionally. The user rejected it: "the evicting the incumbent thing should be a config the consumer can specify, and don't just do it willy-nilly" — and directed me to find where eviction had already been planned/implemented for cc-pool/fusekit, bring those considerations in, and migrate the consumers onto the daemonkit version.

**Why:** Destructive lifecycle behavior (killing an incumbent daemon) has fleet-wide blast radius, and the fleet has usually already thought about it somewhere — a plan, commit message, cc-notes record, or session transcript. Re-deriving the design from scratch discards those considerations and produces unconditional behavior where consumers need control.

**How to apply:** (1) Any daemonkit behavior that can kill/evict/disrupt a consumer's process must be gated by explicit consumer config (opt-in, e.g. a policy field on the config struct), never made the default silently. (2) Before designing daemon lifecycle behavior, search the fleet's prior art first — repo git logs, cc-notes (`ccn search`), past plan files under ~/.cc-pool/accounts/*/plans/, and session transcripts (cc-transcript CLI) — and carry the found considerations into the plan with cites. (3) When daemonkit absorbs a behavior a consumer hand-rolled, the plan must include deleting the consumer's custom code, not leaving both.
